branchingScenario.title: PY3.1-12 | Nerve and Muscle Physiology — Clinical Case

startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: A 28-year-old woman presents with fluctuating weakness and ptosis that worsens throughout the day. Work through this case exploring neuromuscular transmission, the sliding filament theory, and nerve conduction.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You correctly identified the physiology of neuromuscular transmission and demonstrated understanding of muscle contraction and nerve conduction.
You showed some understanding but missed key concepts. Review ACh receptors, the sliding filament mechanism, and saltatory conduction.
Revisit neuromuscular junction physiology, calcium-troponin interaction, myelination, and the all-or-none law.

params.text:
Case Presentation

A 28-year-old woman presents with drooping eyelids and difficulty swallowing that worsens as the day progresses. She reports that her symptoms improve after rest. Anti-acetylcholine receptor antibodies are positive.

This is myasthenia gravis — an autoimmune disorder targeting the neuromuscular junction. The neurotransmitter at the NMJ is acetylcholine, which normally binds to nicotinic receptors on the motor end plate, triggering muscle contraction.

In this patient, antibodies block and destroy these receptors, reducing the effectiveness of neuromuscular transmission.

branchingQuestion.question: In normal muscle contraction (sliding filament theory), what happens when calcium is released into the sarcoplasm?

alternatives.text: Calcium binds to troponin C, which moves tropomyosin away from actin binding sites, allowing cross-bridge formation
Calcium binds directly to actin, causing it to shorten and contract

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: Calcium does not bind directly to actin. It binds to troponin C, which shifts tropomyosin to expose actin binding sites for myosin cross-bridge formation.

alternatives.text: Calcium binds to myosin, activating its ATPase to generate force

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Calcium's primary role is binding to troponin C, not directly to myosin. This shifts tropomyosin off the actin binding sites, permitting the actin-myosin cross-bridge cycle.

params.text:
Further Investigation

Correct! Calcium → troponin C → tropomyosin moves → actin exposed → cross-bridges form.

Nerve conduction studies are performed. The results show a decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation, confirming the NMJ defect. You note that normal myelinated nerve fibres conduct impulses rapidly because of saltatory conduction, where impulses jump between nodes of Ranvier.

The all-or-none law states that a stimulus must reach threshold to generate an action potential, and increasing stimulus strength does not increase the amplitude of the action potential.

branchingQuestion.question: Why do myelinated nerves conduct faster than unmyelinated ones?

alternatives.text: Saltatory conduction: the impulse jumps between nodes of Ranvier, skipping myelinated segments
Myelin increases the amplitude of the action potential at each point

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: The all-or-none law means amplitude is constant regardless of myelin. Myelinated fibres are faster because of saltatory conduction — impulses jump between nodes of Ranvier.

alternatives.text: Myelin provides additional neurotransmitter release at each node

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Neurotransmitters are released at synapses, not along the axon. Myelin speeds conduction via saltatory conduction — the impulse jumps from one node of Ranvier to the next.

params.text:
Clinical Synthesis

Excellent! Key physiological principles from this case:

  • NMJ: ACh binds nicotinic receptors on motor end plate
  • Sliding filament: Ca²⁺ → troponin C → tropomyosin shifts → actin-myosin cross-bridges
  • Saltatory conduction: Impulses jump between nodes of Ranvier in myelinated fibres
  • All-or-none law: Threshold must be reached; amplitude is constant regardless of stimulus strength
  • Myasthenia gravis: Anti-AChR antibodies reduce NMJ transmission efficiency
